title: Trauma and the Body's Pace: Working With Rather Than Against Your Healing
excerpt: Explore why honoring your body's natural pace in trauma healing is crucial for lasting recovery and nervous system regulation.
body:
Understanding Trauma Beyond the Mind

For many who have experienced trauma, the healing journey feels like a battle against their own bodies. Despite therapy and self-work, sensations, triggers, and emotional flashbacks often persist, leaving a sense of being stuck. This disconnect arises because trauma lives not just in the mind but deeply embedded in the body’s nervous system. As Bessel van der Kolk famously said in The Body Keeps the Score, “Trauma is not what happens to you, but what happens inside you as a result.”

Healing trauma effectively requires working with the body's natural rhythms and signals — not pushing against them. In this article, we explore why pacing is essential to trauma recovery, how the nervous system holds trauma, and practical approaches to honor your body’s timing for sustainable healing.

Why the Body’s Pace Matters in Trauma Recovery

When trauma strikes, the autonomic nervous system (ANS) reacts instantly, often bypassing conscious awareness. Stephen Porges’s Polyvagal Theory explains how the nervous system toggles between states of safety, mobilization (fight/flight), and immobilization (freeze/shutdown) in response to threat. Trauma often traps the body in dysregulated states, causing persistent hyperarousal or hypoarousal.

Trying to force healing too quickly or ignore bodily signals can retraumatize the system. As trauma expert Peter Levine explains in Waking the Tiger, trauma is stuck energy in the body that wants to move but cannot. The healing process is about completing the natural defensive responses in a safe, manageable way — which requires patience and attunement to your body’s pace.

“The body holds the score of trauma — healing unfolds when we listen, respect, and move with its tempo rather than against it.” — The Oracle Lover

How Trauma Disrupts Nervous System Regulation

Dan Siegel’s concept of window of tolerance is critical to understanding pacing. This window represents the zone where you can tolerate and integrate emotional and physiological experiences without becoming overwhelmed or shutting down. Trauma narrows this window, making it hard to stay present with challenging feelings or sensations.

When pushed outside this window, the nervous system either hyperactivates (anxiety, panic, rage) or hypoactivates (numbness, dissociation). Francine Shapiro’s work with EMDR demonstrates that healing requires staying within or gently expanding this window, respecting the body’s limits.

Working With the Body: Somatic Approaches to Pacing

Somatic therapies prioritize felt sense and bodily awareness as paths to healing, rather than intellectual analysis alone. Pat Ogden and Peter Levine emphasize tracking sensations and impulses without judgment, allowing the nervous system to discharge trauma safely.

Key principles include:

  • Tracking Sensations: Notice subtle shifts in breath, muscle tension, or temperature to gauge nervous system state.
  • Pendulation: Moving attention between pleasant and challenging sensations to avoid overwhelm.
  • Resourcing: Tapping into internal or external supports to create safety.
  • Allowing Spontaneous Movement: Supporting the body’s natural urges to move or shake as part of releasing stuck energy.

Deb Dana’s work offers practical guidance on using the Polyvagal framework to track nervous system states and respond with compassion and curiosity — not force.

The Danger of Forcing Healing: Why “Quick Fixes” Backfire

Many trauma survivors have tried rapid interventions or pushed themselves to “just get over it.” This approach often leads to retraumatization, as the nervous system cannot integrate experiences outside its capacity. Gabor Maté warns against ignoring the body’s wisdom, emphasizing that trauma recovery is non-linear and must honor individual timing.

Trauma healing isn’t about erasing memories or rushing through emotions but facilitating the nervous system’s natural completion of defensive responses. When ignored or overridden, trauma can manifest as chronic health issues, emotional dysregulation, or relational difficulties.

Practical Tips to Honor Your Healing Pace

Here’s how to work with, rather than against, your body’s pace in trauma recovery:

  • Listen to Your Body: Cultivate mindfulness of sensations without judgment. Use journaling or body scans to track changes.
  • Respect Your Window of Tolerance: If you feel overwhelmed or shut down, step back and engage in grounding activities.
  • Use Somatic Exercises: Practices like grounding, breathwork, gentle movement, or shaking can help discharge stuck energy.
  • Establish Safety: Create internal and external resources like safe places, trusted relationships, or comforting objects.
  • Be Patient: Healing unfolds in stages — progress may feel slow but is meaningful.
  • Work with Skilled Practitioners: Somatic therapists trained in trauma can guide pacing safely.

Integrating Internal Family Systems (IFS) With Somatic Healing

Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems model complements somatic work by identifying internal parts that hold trauma and conflicting needs. These parts often resist healing efforts out of protective intent. Recognizing and gently dialoguing with these parts aligns with pacing principles — honoring all aspects of self without forcing change.

IFS encourages curiosity and compassion, helping survivors integrate fragmented parts at their own speed. Combined with somatic awareness, this approach deepens nervous system regulation and self-trust.

Conclusion: Your Body is the Ultimate Guide

Trauma recovery is less about intellectual insight and more about somatic attunement. The nervous system remembers what the mind forgets, and healing demands patience, respect, and gentle pacing. As Peter Levine reminds us, trauma healing is a dance — not a race. It’s about listening deeply to your body’s rhythms and moving with them, not against them.

When you honor your body’s pace, you create the essential conditions for the nervous system to safely release trauma, expand your window of tolerance, and reclaim your vitality. This is the foundation for sustainable transformation and lasting wholeness.

References

  • Bessel van der Kolk, The Body Keeps the Score, 2014.
  • Peter Levine, Waking the Tiger, 1997.
  • Stephen Porges, The Polyvagal Theory, 2011.
  • Deb Dana, The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy, 2018.
  • Pat Ogden, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, 2006.
  • Gabor Maté, When the Body Says No, 2003.
  • Dan Siegel, The Developing Mind, 1999.
  • Francine Shapiro, EMDR Therapy, 2018.
  • Richard Schwartz, Internal Family Systems Therapy, 1995.

faqs.q: Why is pacing important in trauma healing?
faqs.a: Pacing respects the nervous system's capacity to process trauma safely, preventing overwhelm or shutdown. It allows natural completion of defensive responses, essential for lasting recovery.

faqs.q: How does the nervous system hold trauma?
faqs.a: Trauma disrupts autonomic nervous system regulation, trapping the body in states of hyperarousal or hypoarousal. These states manifest as physical sensations and emotional responses that persist after the event.

faqs.q: What are somatic techniques for working with trauma?
faqs.a: Somatic techniques include tracking bodily sensations, pendulation, resourcing, breathwork, and allowing spontaneous movement. These help release stuck energy and restore nervous system balance.

faqs.q: Can I do trauma healing work on my own?
faqs.a: Yes, self-directed work is possible but should be done gently and patiently. Using somatic awareness and grounding techniques can help, though working with trained professionals is recommended for complex trauma.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book that explains how trauma is stored in the body and offers science-backed approaches to healing.

metaTitle: Trauma Healing: Work With Your Body’s Natural Pace
metaDescription: Discover why honoring your body's pace is vital for trauma recovery and learn somatic methods to heal effectively without overwhelm.
author: The Oracle Lover
